title: Restricted CSV stores integrity hashes (Md5/Sha256) that disagree with the protected payload
description: Restricted-config CSVs (e.g. config/kill-switches.csv) carry per-row Md5 and Sha256 columns alongside the secret payload (Passphrase). If the payload is mutated and the hashes are not recomputed — or vice versa — the row is silently corrupt: any defender that relies on hash-equality for tamper detection sees nothing wrong, while the runtime sees a stale or attacker-substituted secret. This pattern requires every load of such a CSV to verify hash agreement and raise on drift.
detection_method: For each row in any CSV under config/ that exposes both a payload column AND Md5/Sha256 columns, recompute hashes of the UTF-8 bytes of the payload and compare to stored values (case-insensitive lowercase hex). Any mismatch is a finding. Also flag rows where Md5/Sha256 are missing/empty when the schema declares them.
preventionRule: Never mutate a hash-bound CSV payload column without recomputing the hash columns in the same write. Provide a single mutator API (e.g. Set-VersionKillSwitchPassphrase) that recomputes Md5/Sha256 transactionally and rejects manual edits. On every module load, call Test-KillSwitchIntegrity (or equivalent) and refuse to register handlers if drift is detected.
remedy: Add Test-KillSwitchIntegrity (or *-CsvIntegrity) function. On load: compute md5=lower(hex(MD5(utf8(Passphrase)))) and sha256=lower(hex(SHA256(utf8(Passphrase)))) and assert equal to stored values. On any write path that touches Passphrase, recompute Md5/Sha256 in the same Export-Csv call. Add a Pester test that mutates a temp CSV and asserts the verifier returns drift findings.

first_instance.fix_summary: Added Test-KillSwitchIntegrity exported function; computes MD5 and SHA256 of Passphrase column and compares to stored Md5/Sha256; returns array of {Version,Field,Expected,Actual} drift records (empty = OK). Pester smoke covers happy-path + tamper case.

examples_bad: Passphrase column changed manually in CSV editor; Md5/Sha256 still hash the old value
examples_good: Use Set-VersionKillSwitchPassphrase that overwrites Passphrase + recomputes Md5/Sha256 in a single Export-Csv UTF8
